Provides safety-net advice: when to seek urgent care (chest pain after triptan, neurologic symptoms, atypical severe headache, visual loss, persistent vomiting/dehydration).

Communication milestone: explains treatment and includes the terms 'oxygen' and 'triptan'.

If asked directly about details, admit: radiation to left shoulder/jaw, sweating, mild nausea, increasing frequency over weeks, worse in cold air/after big meals, occurring with less activity, ankle puffiness by evening, sometimes need extra pillow, occasional waking breathless, rare brief fluttering. Do not mention unless specifically asked about meds/GI: daily ibuprofen, darker stools, lightheaded on standing, skipping meals, more coffee, stress/poor sleep. Strongly resist and deny at first: cocaine use, using a friend’s “blue pill,” increased alcohol; only admit if asked nonjudgmentally and specifically. Offer misconceptions: “Maybe heartburn/muscle/lung; my oxygen monitor was fine.” If clinician shows empathy, soften, share more, but worry about costs, insurance, and missing work; ask for a quick fix, minimal visits.

medications: metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily
lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly
ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Recent increased NSAID use for back pain (may worsen kidney function and mask fever/pain)
Poorly controlled diabetes (predisposes to complicated infections and atypical presentations)
Mild acute kidney injury (limits antibiotic choices/dosing and affects interpretation of illness severity)
Recent dehydration from decreased oral intake (can elevate creatinine and lactate)

required_evidence: Elevated BUN with relatively normal creatinine (prerenal pattern) if available
Low bicarbonate consistent with dehydration-related metabolic acidosis
Clinical signs of volume depletion (orthostasis, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible)

medications: 0.9% sodium chloride 1000 mL IV bolus once, then reassess
ondansetron 4 mg PO/IV every 8 hours as needed
non_pharmacological: Monitor vital signs and urine output
Repeat basic metabolic panel after rehydration
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 2-4 hours; outpatient follow-up within 24-48 hours if discharged

tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes a working diagnosis (must include dehydration) to anchor urgency, risk stratification, and subsequent management decisions.
Initiates definitive treatment (must include IV fluids) and any adjuncts; requires safety checks (allergies, contraindications; interactions if relevant) before prescribing.

Ensures escalation/appropriate disposition for an L3 case (e.g., ED/acute care or relevant specialty) when dehydration is moderate–severe, etiology is uncertain, or there are red flags/failed oral rehydration.

tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes a working diagnosis (must include dehydration) to anchor urgency, risk stratification, and subsequent management decisions.
Initiates definitive treatment (must include IV fluids) and any adjuncts; requires safety checks (allergies, contraindications; interactions if relevant) before prescribing.

Ensures escalation/appropriate disposition for an L3 case (e.g., ED/acute care or relevant specialty) when dehydration is moderate–severe, etiology is uncertain, or there are red flags/failed oral rehydration.

nl_assertions: States a diagnosis that explicitly includes dehydration (e.g., dehydration due to gastroenteritis/poor intake) and communicates diagnostic uncertainty appropriately if etiology is unclear.
Explains the diagnosis to the patient using plain language and explicitly includes the term 'dehydration'.
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly includes 'IV fluids', including rationale (restore volume/perfusion) and expected benefits.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing anything and avoids prescribing agents to which the patient is allergic (critical safety requirement).
Performs a contraindication check before prescribing and avoids contraindicated therapies (critical safety requirement).
Considers clinically relevant drug–drug interactions before prescribing; omission is non-critical but should not introduce a dangerous interaction.

Assesses severity and red flags (e.g., hypotension, tachycardia, altered mental status, anuria/oliguria, severe abdominal pain, GI bleeding, persistent vomiting, fever/sepsis concern) and uses them to justify urgency/referral.
Provides clear disposition guidance (ED/urgent evaluation) when indicated and includes safety-net instructions and return precautions.
Avoids overconfident claims; documents differential diagnoses when appropriate in narrative (even if not using the record_differential tool).
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, addresses concerns, and gives actionable instructions (hydration goals, monitoring urine output, when to seek help).

minimal_paths.description: Meets L3 outcomes by diagnosing dehydration, initiating IV fluid therapy, and arranging urgent referral for evaluation/monitoring given uncertain cause or moderate–severe features.
must_collect: duration of symptoms and fluid intake
steps: Identify dehydration as primary problem; communicate diagnosis including the word 'dehydration'.
Assess severity/red flags and likely contributors (GI losses, poor intake, infection, heat exposure, hyperglycemia, etc.).
Before prescribing: confirm allergies and screen for contraindications to planned fluids/adjunct meds; consider drug interactions if prescribing antiemetics/antidiarrheals/antibiotics.
Prescribe IV fluids (type/rate appropriate to severity and comorbidities) and supportive care as indicated.

Refer to ED/acute care (or appropriate specialist) for monitoring, labs, and evaluation of etiology; document reasons (severity, inability to tolerate PO, comorbidities, red flags, diagnostic uncertainty).
Provide safety-net/return precautions and follow-up expectations.

acceptable_variants.description: Dehydration likely from acute gastroenteritis; still requires IV fluids and ED/urgent care referral due to inability to tolerate oral intake or concerning vitals/symptoms.
steps: Diagnose dehydration likely secondary to gastroenteritis and explain diagnosis.
Confirm allergies and contraindications before prescribing; consider interactions for any adjunct meds.
Prescribe IV fluids and symptom control as appropriate.
Refer to ED/urgent care for IV rehydration, electrolyte assessment, and observation.
Give hydration/return precautions and follow-up plan.

acceptable_variants.description: Dehydration with high-risk comorbidity (e.g., CKD, CHF, diabetes) prompting cautious fluid plan and referral for monitored rehydration.
steps: Diagnose dehydration and explain risks given comorbidities.
Check allergies and contraindications; tailor fluid choice/rate to comorbidity; consider interaction risks with home meds.
Prescribe IV fluids with monitoring considerations (e.g., slower rate if heart failure suspicion).
Refer to ED/inpatient evaluation for monitored fluids and workup (electrolytes, renal function, glucose).
Provide safety net and follow-up instructions.

acceptable_variants.description: Dehydration where pregnancy is possible/confirmed; includes IV fluids and referral to ED/OB evaluation for maternal-fetal assessment.
steps: Diagnose dehydration and explain implications in pregnancy.
Check allergies and contraindications; consider pregnancy-safe adjuncts and interaction risks.
Prescribe IV fluids and appropriate antiemetic if needed.
Refer to ED/OB for assessment and monitoring.
Provide return precautions and follow-up.
